speaker: interviewer
text: There-- is the tape on?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's not running.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. They shipped all the Jews out. The left about 80 or 90 people-- tailors, painters that they needed to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: In [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Yeah, if you'll tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. OK. I just wanted to make sure that I didn't miss something when you were telling your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm thinking as I'm talking if I'm not missing anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can see the wheels turning.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is tape number two with Sol-- S-O-L-- Liber-- L-I-B-E-R-- on Thursday, August 11, 1994, in Beverly Hills, California. And my name is Merle Goldberg. OK? When we came into the ghetto, there was no apartments. We moved in with another family. A couple had a small child-- about four or five-year-old child. My mother was still kosher. Those people were already a long time in the ghetto. They were cooking-- they were cooking already whatever they could get. So my mother used to put in-- like a bort-- it shouldn't splash from the other side to her side of the stove, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after a few months, it didn't work, you know. We were all the same, in the same boat, you know? And there was nothing to do. We tried to get out to work outside the ghetto, maybe. And then you needed some preferential treatment from groups, you know. Oh, they always did somebody-- for somebody they knew, or they were close to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, I said that-- to my family-- I was always the initiator, you know, the youngest of the brothers. I said, I cannot stay here. If there's nothing to do, you can't work. You can't live. I saw people dying in the street. Every few minutes, somebody was covered with a paper. And this was the whole day. This was not just one or two. Hundreds.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother had a leather suitcase-- a leather case, actually. He went out-- everybody was selling what you could-- what they-- valuables that they could get a few zlotys for. He went and sold it for 5 zlotys-- 5 zlotys was the equivalent to $1. And he gave it to me. He said, you want to go, go. I took this money, and the next morning I jumped over the fence. It was on [NON-ENGLISH]-- if somebody will ever see it, maybe he'll know where it was. The city-- the street was-- was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what was the street? [NON-ENGLISH]-- it was separated. In the middle of the street, one was the ghetto on one side. And the other half was on the Polish side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I jumped over the fence. I was lucky that there was nobody around, because there was an order, if you deliver a Jew, you're going to get a kilo of sugar-- two and a half pounds of sugar. There was nobody there, and-- and another boy talked down, and he said he also wants to go from the same city. He wants to go, so, OK, we both went. And we marched close to the road, we shouldn't get lost, to the main road. And we walked in one day-- actually, two days, we made it to Grojec. We slept over-- I don't remember where we slept over the night. We came to Grojec. He lived in a-- in a street that there were two brothers-- blacksmiths. And they were communists. When we came there, they saw us. They said, look-- you know me. I'm not going to take you to the Germans, but if anybody else sees you here, it wouldn't take them even two seconds to take you to the Germans. They gave us some bread and tell us, march on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We marched on. It took us another day to get to Bialobrzegi. I had a-- two cousins in Bialobrzegi. When I came to Bialobrzegi-- this is actually the picture that I showed you. He lived in Bialobrzegi. And when we came to Bialobrzegi, he said, what are you doing here? It's the same mess here as in Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only people-- the only way people survive here is they go to the villages and beg for some food. There's no work. There's nothing. And I saw the mess, also. People are walking the street. They fell-- they walk and fell dead. Just actually-- just like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how long-- he let me sleep on the floor. He actually was with his in-laws, still living there. And I slept on the floor and-- look, how long can you sit? They didn't have anything, themselves, so-- I went. I started going to the villages, but I had to cross a bridge. It's the second biggest waterway in Poland. It's called Pilica.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were girls-- German girls on the bridge from either side. I walked out the first day, and my stomach was working, because I knew if the Germans catch you, they shoot you. They shot a lot of people. And as I walked out, I had a hat-- I went out, and I saw a guy-- also a poor guy, was going around there. And he was wearing a hat from the gymnasium. And I asked, let's exchange it so I'm not going to look Jewish. And also, those hats were-- they were wearing-- not today the guys in college that they act up-- was in those days to-- every year they finished a year of school, they used to could make two cuts in their hat, you know. So the hat was-- had quite a few cuts, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took his hat, and I walked to the village. And you used to say, praise God, you know, in Polish, you know, [POLISH]. And they asked-- some of them asked you, where's your family? Where's your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, they shipped us out from Silesia. I don't know where my parents are. I'm alone-- they shipped us apart. They shipped in a different direction [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, they felt sorry for me. They gave me some food. And I walked around there all day, and I came home. Some days I came home with a lot of food. So bread, potatoes, anything you could get to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, one day, my father-- I walk out to the village. I see my father on the road. I say, what are you do-- how do you come here? He says he also escaped. So I had two in the family already here. So-- and this was going on-- and my mother came, and my younger sister came. My two brothers were working in a factory out of Warsaw for the Germans, and my sisters were working on a big farm, also, out of-- around outskirts of Warsaw. Anyway, I walked to these villages for-- everyday you had to change your village. You couldn't go every day to the same people, you see? One day my mother said, she wants to go with me. I say, the only thing is it's going to be-- for me is a hardship, because they'll recognize you as a middle-aged person, and you wouldn't be able to keep up with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She insisted she wants to go. So I say, OK. You want to go, you can go.

speaker: interviewee
text: We go across the bridge. We go into the first village. We encounter four Polish people and a policeman. Where are you going? You know, you're Jews. You're carrying-- you're carrying the typhoid fever here to the-- our villages. And they're going to take us to the Germans. I recognized the policeman. When I was an apprentice, my boss used to make uniforms for the police. And he was one of them. He was a policeman in our city. And we-- I called him by the name. I say, what are you going to gain if you take us? We'll go back. If you don't want us here, we'll go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the other four-- they want to take us to the Germans. He actually couldn't look in my face. You know, he knew-- I told him where I worked, and I told him his name and everything. They walked aside, and they made up their mind they're going to take us across the water. And I thought they were going to take us across-- they took us three quarters across, and they pushed us in the water. I don't swim, and my mother doesn't swim. And it's-- it's a big waterway-- even three quarters across, it's still deep.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- it must have been a miracle. I don't believe in miracles, but it must have been a miracle. I hold my mother's hand with one hand. I was splashing like-- you see some-- a doggy splashing in the water? That's how we went-- we came to the end of the waterway.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came out, we-- we were all-- we were both wet. We had no other clothes to change. My mother was shivering like a fish out of the water. And we sat there two hours, till-- because we couldn't walk into the city dripping wet. So we sat there about two hours. The wind dried us out a little bit. And we came home with nothing. Nothing to eat, nothing-- wet. It's funny now-- it wasn't funny then. And I said to my parents, I have to move on again. There's-- there's nothing I can do here. And I decided that I'll go and look for some work on the farm. I went out. I came out to the first village. I went into a farm. You know, and you can't-- in Poland-- I don't know here. I've never been to a village. Lunch time-- they used to have wooden stoves. You could smell the food-- the whole village, you could smell cooking.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked into one farm. I say, I'm hungry. I need some food. I'll work for you. He says, a Jewish boy cannot work on a farm. I'll say, why don't you try me? Don't give me the food before I do some work. He says, OK. I'll go and eat lunch. And he gave me a pitchfork and say, if you'll throw out some manure from the stable, you'll get lunch. I went in there-- only sparks were going, you know? I wanted to show them that I could work, because I was used to it. We had horses so I knew how to operate.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came-- he came out after lunch. He crossed himself. He couldn't believe it. He couldn't see the door from the stable. He came over, he says, I don't believe it. He called his-- he called his wife, Mama, give the little Jew some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: She brought out a plate, you know, with potatoes and some borscht, and all this, and a slice of bread. I ate. He said, if you want to stay, I'll keep you as long as I need you. I said, fine. You can sleep in the-- in the-- what do you call it? Where you keep the hay-- in a-- In the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barn?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the silo, the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I worked there about three months until the fall came around. In the wintertime, they had no work. You know, the fields are plowed and the seed and-- the only work they have around the house, they could do it themselves. I had to march on again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I walked to the next village. It took a few miles. I saw a rich kid. He was in the same class with me in school-- from a rich family. He was herding cows. He didn't want to look in my face. He was afraid to, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went into one farmer-- the same story. You're not going to be able to-- how could you? Jews don't-- Jews don't work on a farm. Actually, we had Jewish farmers around our city-- plenty. I say, what do you have to lose? You try me, and if I-- if it doesn't work out, you tell me to go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, he tells me, harness the horses. You know, the Polish help takes one horse, puts it into the wagon, takes the other horse. With us, we did-- we had different systems, you know. To do it faster, I harness-- I took both horses out in the same time. I harnessed them. It took me split seconds. Same story, you know. He couldn't believe it. OK, you-- the other help-- the main help-- his name was Bolek. He said, you'll go with him. They used to pick up manure from the gendarmes, at the-- in the forest. It had stables. So they paid two zlotys-- like a half-- half a dollar. And they picked up as much as they could load in a wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- I went with them, and we loaded our wagon of manure. We came back. He gave us lunch. And he told us after we finished lunch, we shall go again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, tell me, could you give me permission to load this wagon really-- I'll bring you a double wagon for the same money, because this is not enough, I say.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, how are you going to do it? I say, if you give me permission, I'll come back with our wagon-- you'll see. I went, and I told the other guys-- I say, first we'll load inside the wagon. Then we'll make a pyramid over the wagon. Come to a pyramid-- I came back with the wagon. The horses couldn't pull it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, he talked to God, you know. He has a double wagon here for the same price. Well, I worked in this place quite for a long time. I actually plowed, and I learned how, and I-- all work in the farm-- whatever. And one day, a farmer's-- a poor farmer-- the house burned down right across from where I worked. And they helped each other, so he said I should go with the wagon and help bring home some logs to build another house. I went, and they stayed-- the other farmer stayed. They don't know how to load it. And I turned the wagon sideways and put chains. And the horses pulled the-- the-- the logs that the wagon came back straight by itself. It fell into the wagon actually. They couldn't believe it-- what's happening. It took me about a half an hour to load all the wagons. I came back, and it went around the village that this farmer has a Jewish boy that is just unbelievable. He knows everything. Everybody wants to hire me. But then after-- right after that, there-- there came some-- they distributed some leaflets to the farmers that anybody who will hire-- keep a Jew-- the family will be shot. He came over to me. He says, I love-- I love your work, and I would like you to work, but I cannot take a chance with my family. See, read it yourself. Well, I, myself, don't blame him, you know. I didn't blame him, because he has to protect himself. Some took chances, but not-- very few.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left to get me some money, get me some food, a sack full of whatever-- edible things. And I knew my brother was working not far away on a railroad station fixing the railroad-- what do you call it? The ties?

speaker: interviewee
text: The railroad lines-- they used to put--

speaker: interviewer
text: The railroad ties.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I came there. Half the people have typhoid fever already. They're asleep on-- on the floor, on straw. You have about 40 people sleeping on the floor. I had nowhere to go. I had to take a chance. I start working there, and I became a-- a good worker, I was. Right away, the Germans recognized, and they took me to be the head of the group. But every day, somebody else died, you know. Well, it came to my brother-- he-- he caught the typhoid fever. So I had some money from the farm yet. So I took him in-- it was a few kilometers from a small town-- and a farmer was going to the city of Radom. I don't know if you interviewed people. Maybe you heard about the city. It's a big city. And I gave the farmer some money. He took him in the wagon and took him into Radom. And he went to the hospital. There was already a ghetto. There were so many people coming into the hospital with typhoid fever. Very few survived. He died after a couple of days. Then I came down with it. I came down with it-- I went, like I considered home. My parents were living in a-- considered a shack, because he had no money to pay rent, so whatever you could get, you moved in. Let me think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, I came back. And I told them, I'm sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this affect all of them, and the death of your brother? How did this all affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked my father to come and pick up the clothes. So he walked quite a way. But he had some friends on the way that also he used to meet in Warsaw on the market. So he stopped over there and had some food. And how it affect-- that it was the first casualty in our family. And it affected us all. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came down with it. There was no food. I was-- I had such a high temperature that I was talking out of my head, you know. My mother told me later. The only thing she kept giving me is hot water. And she had some alcohol-- rubdowns with alcohol and cold water to keep the temperature down. And I survived. When I survived-- I-- I came out of it. I couldn't work, I was so weak. I couldn't get out. I couldn't stand on my feet. My father was working. There was so many to bury, he was digging graves for the dead people. And that's how he got some money. But the-- it was very little for-- we were four people. Then my oldest brother came in. They told him to leave-- to dissolve this place and they don't need him anymore. So he came in. So we were five already in the shack. His boss that he used to work in Grojec happened to be there, also, so he hired them. And he did some work for him. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One day, he went to work, and the SS picked him up to go to work. And he says to me, I'm still working. Excuse me. I'll take a little bit of water if you don't mind. [DRINKS] I'm still working. I could still help my family. And maybe you'll go for me. So he asked the SS man if he could-- if I could go for him. He says, I don't care as long as I have a person to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went. They took us four kilometers from Bialobrzegi. It was a big ranch. And they assigned me to work in the stables to clean horses. They gave me four horses-- white horses to clean. I couldn't even reach up to the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave you a brush and a scrub to clean the horses. You clean the horses every morning. Let's see. You got up 5 o'clock. You took him for an inspection. They were wearing white gloves. Go against the grain. Every time, I got a beating. They're not clean enough. This was going on for weeks, you know. I was restless again, because if you stay in a situation like this, you know that the end will come. They'll beat you to death. And one day, I was so tired from the beating and getting up early, and not getting enough food, I put my hand on the trough. And I put one foot on the pitchfork that I used to clean the stable. And I dozed off. And a [NON-ENGLISH] came in with a rope to go and bring hay for the horses, and he saw me dozing off, and he hit me with the heavy side-- I-- this was a-- a cord that you harnessed horses and a heavy end, you know. He hit me over the head, and I-- like, I was startled at first, and also like in a daze. Not till I came to, he tried to hit me again. And I took the pitchfork, and I put it in his belly. Yeah. And I left it there. I opened the door to the back field, and I escaped. I escaped-- I came in the outskirts of the city. My mother had a cousin-- he was a roofer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walk over, and I say, Shlomo, give me an armband. Because without an armband, you're dead. He says, you-- you want to tell me you are here? You know, the whole city's-- the whole-- every German, every SS is looking for you. The-- the man died. The [NON-ENGLISH] died.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, don't give me questions and answers. If they catch me, it's too bad. Give me an armband. They gave me an armband, and I walked on the outskirts to my parents' place. They said, the SS just left. They were looking for you. I say, I'm surprised they didn't take you as hostages. My sister was there. The parents were there. And I said to my parents, I'm going back to Warsaw where my sisters are working. I have nowhere else to go. My mother says she wants to go with me. I say the same thing like you went to the village. You're just going to hold me back, because it's 77 kilometers. It's not easy for a middle-aged woman to walk. Well, I talked her out of it. Besides, it was dangerous for me to cross the bridge. There was guards on the bridge and they doubled the guard. I sat there-- not far away-- I knew a person there, and I was sitting at their house until the guard changed. While the guard changed, I went through the bridge. And I walked in to a village 4 kilometers before Grojec that we-- my father used to rent orchards from the guy. And I called him by the name, and I told him who I am. He says, look. He says, I know you're Sana's son. I recognize you. You see, I have six people working-- we just picking the grain from the fields. I could keep you only over the night. I say, that's all I need. But you have to sleep in the other side from the-- from those people, because if they knew you're Jewish, they'll take you to the German like it's night now. Well, I went to sleep, and I slept through the night. 4 o'clock, he woke me up in the morning. He brought me a half a bread. They usually bake their own bread-- the farmers. He said, may God be with you, in Polish. And I left. I left, and I walked actually with-- next-- usually walked next to the road, that I shouldn't get lost. Germans were marching recruits, you know, limping, you know. And I encounter an SS man and a volksdeutsch-- the biggest-- the biggest bastard you ever saw. I'm going to have to tell it, even on the-- on the tape. And they're riding horses. And I ditched, they shouldn't see me. When they passed, I walked on. I walked around-- I didn't go into the city. I walked around it. I was afraid to walk into the city. I walked halfway to Warsaw-- almost half-- no, almost half a day. But nightfall, you have to stay somewhere. I walked in, and I said the same thing-- praise God in Polish, you know. And they said, you could stay overnight. And they gave me some food. And I helped them to finish off their chores at-- in the evening. In the morning, I left, and I walked again. I had to make 25, 30 kilometers a day. I walked till I had the same thing. Nightfall, I had to go somewhere. I went into a village, and same thing-- I told them I'll help them, and they should let me stay overnight. It was Saturday night. Sunday morning, all the people are going to church. And this was scary, you know. It's-- it's-- I had to go to the right to go to the road-- I had to go left, make a stretch, you know, because I was afraid to go through those people.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day came-- by the nightfall, I came into Warsaw. You see the merry-go-rounds, children playing. You feel bad. Yeah, you-- you feel so downtrodden, and hear people enjoying themselves. And when you're young, you take risks. Don't you think I took the streetcar?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took the streetcar, because it was a long way. It's in the outskirts of Warsaw. Germans were with Polish girls in the streetcar, laughing, joking, you know. And I was standing and looking out the window. Nobody should see me. It was Sunday. I came to the gate where my sisters were working. Nobody was working. They were sitting-- they were sitting next to the fence. There were two sisters also working there from our city. She noticed me, and she yelled to my sisters-- your brother's here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Your brother's here. They looked around. They couldn't do anything. They told me, wait til nightfall. I'm thinking back if I'm not missing out anything. I waited til nightfall. They took me in, they gave me some food and asked me questions. They didn't know if you're alive, even. They asked me questions, and they say that the foreman is a German. And he doesn't let one more Jew come in here. I was hidden in a bed covered up for two weeks. I didn't get out. A whole day, I was lying in a bed covered up over my face, over my head with a blanket, till somebody squealed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop here so we can change the tape.
